All right, step 17 of the 17 steps in the simple writing system is your close. By close we mean finalizing the deal. If you're selling something, then money exchanges hands. If you're asking for some other action, then that action takes place. So the close involves the dichotomy of the heartbreak of selling from your heels, which you don't want to do, versus wealth beyond the dreams of avarice. If you remember, in a previous step I talked about people selling from their heels. They've kind of got one foot behind them and saying, do you want this? You probably don't. Yeah, I didn't think so. I suck at selling. And they bolt. Don't do that. That is bad salesmanship. And if you don't learn and start to incorporate good salesmanship, you're not going to do well in business. You're not going to do well in life either. So there's a lot of benefits to learning the salesmanship we've been talking about. Beginning with wealth beyond the dreams of avarice, which is an old school way of saying that it's the keys to the kingdom. Whatever metaphor you want to talk about, when you get this down, then you're off to the races. That's three metaphors in a row. So I'm bringing up a slide again because it's so important. You've seen this before. You're not having deja vu. But the one-two punch of selling effectively involves the rational reasons to explain why your prospect bought. So he can then go back and explain to everyone else in his life, including himself, when he wakes up in the middle of the night and asks himself, why did I buy that again? So if anyone says, why did you buy that? Or you made a mistake by buying this. He can say, no, I didn't. It's a great bargain. I get all this other stuff. All of this will happen. I'm not risking a penny. So these are rational things that he can say and are easily memorable because you've spelled them out very specifically. And number two, you're balancing this against the emotional eight brain reasons that seal the deal. And that is basically it's filling the basic primitive needs of our brain, which wants more, better, bigger things. Once it now, wants more than the other guy, wants to be the person in charge, wants respect in a world where they're not getting much respect, and wants things to happen to them that are nice and cushy and full of privilege and luxury. They can't quite, some of it's even unconscious, but you need to be aware of what it is that's driving the sale. And it's a combination of the rational reasons. It was a great deal. It was a wonderful bargain. And I got a lot of stuff that I really wanted and I'm not risking anything with the emotional part, which is this makes me feel better about myself. This makes me feel hope for the future. This makes me feel that I will be able to have a better lifestyle with more money and it makes me feel that the things that I was afraid of, like going bankrupt or having bad things happen to me, aren't going to happen. I'm arming myself against a hostile world. So here's an example in four steps. First step. This was copied from one of the ads I wrote for the client who was selling information on how to be a better, I forget if it was a bass player, yeah, it was a bass, how to be a professional level bass player. So this is towards the end of the copy. And actually this became a VSL so it's towards the end of the video. Until now, only a handful of lucky players have discovered these amazing, quote, learn it today, blow away other musicians tonight, secrets of powering any song with a killer pounding groove that drives girls crazy and humiliates competing bands. Now thanks to his breakthrough, quote, speed teaching, unquote, you too can take your bass playing skills from wherever you are now to respected killer pro status in the shortest time humanly possible. Result, audition with unreal confidence, earn massive respect from other musicians and bring home more money from more and better gigs. Best of all, you can check everything out for free if you like. Step two. Here's the deal. This is a drop dead bargain. Rex, that's the name of the teacher. Rex doesn't have time to teach you personally and would set you back huge bucks just to get a private lesson anyway. Plus you'd have to travel to his hometown. What you get in this package is worth literally thousands of dollars, not just in lesson fees, but in all the extra cash that will be available once you finally learn to be the best bass player in town. Demand creates income, dude. Each of these DVDs has been privately priced out at $97, making the package worth at least $291. But right now, because Rex has insisted we keep the price low, you can have it rush shipped to you for just $97. However, you don't risk a penny because you're covered by a complete no hassle guarantee. Step three. Here's what to do now. Use your credit card and call 1-800-blah-blah. That was the ordering method that we used in this particular project. Just say you want the maximum bottom DVD package and we'll take care of everything else. If the number is busy and we expect to be swamped with calls on this, keep calling back. Don't let this opportunity slip away. Or if you want to use a check or money order, payable to OHP, that was the way these guys price thing or got the deal closed. Just clearly type or print out your name and mailing address and the words, I want Rex's maximum bottom DVD package on a piece of paper. And mail it with your payment, blah, blah, blah, to this. We do same day shipping. Again, if you, excuse me, we do same day shipping. Again, you are completely covered with a hyper-generous six months, 100% money back guarantee so you don't risk anything. Just order right now while you're thinking about it. Thanks, Dr. Michael Leary, Prez, OHP Music. And the last step, the PS's. Almost forgot, if you order today, I will include for free a thick tab workbook that spells out all the secrets in the speed lesson. This tab makes everything super simple and easy and even works as a fake book for gigs that you can consult during breaks to make songs work even the first time you play them. PPS, one last thing, just for readers of this magazine, I want you to keep the DVD with the student lesson even if you return the rest of the package for a refund later. It's my gift to you just for giving this amazing package a test drive. Now to explain to people who don't understand exactly what a PS is. PS stands for Post Script and it goes back to the direct mail days where PS is where at the end of the letter or in a print ad, it might be at the end of the advertisement in a magazine. And what it is is an afterthought. It's like we've signed it, we've done the deal, thanks sincerely, Joe Smith, but then you have another thought. So there's PS. The PPS stands for Post Script and sometimes when I would write stuff I would have five or six. I would go Post Post Post Script, Post Post Post Post Script because I kept adding ideas after the fact and what I was doing was piling on at this point. So these four steps are actually closing the deal, then it closes it tighter, then it closes it even tighter and closes it tighter still. So that's the job of those things. These four steps, even though it's for a specific product in a specific market, just take the specifics out and think about how they will work for you, how building the process to the point that the prospect really has no choice at this point if he agrees with you that it's a good price, it's a bargain. Look, I get a lot of this other stuff and if I act now I also get even more stuff and I get free stuff and I can keep it and I have no risk and all this stuff is going on. It's just an overwhelming offer and the prospect will say yes, I'll do it. This was very, very successful. In the PSS I've used the almost forgot and one last thing and I've even said after that, oops, I forgot there's even one more last thing. It's just, it's like in person you might say oh, I almost forgot and there's this too and there's this other stuff and that reminds me there's even more stuff and this is how we actually get to the close. This is how things go from maybe I'll do it to okay, you got me, I'll do it. Each step builds on the one before. You tweak the emotional part of your prospect's brain. You reinforce the rational reasons why and you swing into action. Here's what to do right now. In the example I gave you it was pick up the phone or mail and do all this stuff online. It would just be click on this link here. If it's in an email, it's click on this link, go to the page, read this. Any action you want them to take, this is the time to say this is what to do now and explain it in almost excruciating detail so nothing is left to chance. And the PS coup de gras would be just piling on the last stuff that you have and that's how you close the deal and that is your 17 step formula. So stay frosty. Okay, we're back again, the close. Here's how this works and how it's worked in the real world for thousands, nearly 2,000 people through the simple writing system. By the time people are at this part of the coaching program, they're writing. So it's surprise, we have relatively few questions from students because normally they're just in the home stretch here and they're just putting up examples to get some feedback on. So I, there's a couple of general questions. I'd like to get here, you talk about a little bit just to help people. Again, you're probably, in your own mind, you're probably pretty clear now what you're going to do but let's just see if John, we can pull a couple more tidbits out of you. The examples you use, as you said, were from printed letters and one was then taken to a video and it worked in both formats. So, yeah, because we want you to see what John's talking about, some examples. We're using text examples and not making you sit through long videos. How, what are different ways to close depending on the medium? Let's, I'm going to start with the PS. If you're shooting a video, how does that concept apply as opposed to a written page or a website where it says PS? A lot of things in the written world are not said when used in conversation. So you wouldn't say PS because it doesn't make sense in a video but you would say something like the rest of the stuff which is, oh yeah, one more thing. Because you, the emphasis you put on the words or the way you say it is almost like we're talking and it's like, oh wait, wait, wait, I have one more thing. That would be how a PS is supposed to be read in a written text copy. So it's still the same thing. You're still using the same elements. It's just translated to a more conversational style. So the, all of the things that you see in the written piece like bold and italic, you can't bold something you're saying but you can change the emphasis you're putting on it. Or you can use conversational tactics like, wait, wait, wait, wait, we have something else to talk about, you know, things like that. So using those tactics are great in audio. It's an advantage that you have in video and in audio in that you can use expressions. Audio has an advantage over printed stuff. Video has an advantage over audio because then there are also, you know, movements that you make have an advantage. Being in person with someone has an even better advantage because then all the other senses come into play. So it's just making use of the medium that you have. Okay. In written advertising, most of the time the close is at the end. Whether it was a text-based website, in part because people can jump and they do jump. They're able to, what's this about? You can't stop them from checking the price or the PS, oh, catch the ride. It's a standalone story. But generally, you know, the buy now button or where the order information, the close is at the bottom. Video, it has to be consumed linearly in a linear matter. It's people don't jump 20 minutes and see what you're, but wait, there's more. They have to go through it. So what are some different strategies about closing in videos? And specifically, do it at the end. Do you say if the video is 20 minutes long does someone have to go through 20 minutes or do you give them opportunities to, if you're ready to go now, before I tell you the rest, here's how you order? Well, of course, the main answer is to test. And things have been changing and we know people who have done all kinds of different ways of testing. They will have, it was sticked with the 20 minute video, but we've known people who have done 40 minute videos, hour and a half videos. Five minute videos. So there are different standards of how people respond in different niches within different markets and it will continue to change. So make sure you are hip to what the competition is doing, what other people are doing in other markets, how this is changing. It's just not written in stone and it never has been. So sometimes it's, we've had clients that we've worked with who have done a 20 minute video and the buy button appeared in the 17th minute and they were actually doing the close and you had to go through the entire video. So if anybody wouldn't stick around for the whole video, you're going to lose those people. Other people do that, but they also have the text of what they're saying, a transcript down there. And they still try to control when the pricing information comes out because that's part of the way they're presenting everything in their sales message. But other people are less, are more casual about it and they'll have a buy button under the beginning of the video right from the start even though you don't know how much it is and you're taking off there. In traditional direct mail, of course, the way people would read is they would get a letter, they would see the headline, then they'd flip it over and look at the PS and try to gauge what the price was and what the deal was. And so you wrote it in a way where, I might get some information. If it was a $39 deal, then that would be okay. If it was a $1,000 deal, then you're sending to the wrong prospect. They may toss it in the trash right away, but you were aware that they were skipping around. Online with text-based websites, people do skip around. They scroll way down to the bottom, try to see what the price is. So there's a lot of other visual things that come into play, which is as they're scrolling down towards the bottom, are they seeing the guarantee? Are they seeing, are you actually having a PS? Are you spelling out the deal there? So the job of the headline becomes much more important. We try to get as much information there as you can. Are there testimonials still down there towards the end? A lot of people stack the testimonials up top. Forget that people are jumping down to the bottom. They still need to see those third-party endorsements. So keep all of this in mind and test if you can. Yeah, I would say that we've heard not everything working, but from the, you said, the range of you've got to sit 40 minutes through a video and then the buy button appears and they're doing very well. Other people, because with the metrics you can track, they're gathering data on the number of people they're losing. Yeah. So they have tested, you know, at a 10-minute point, they're going to give an opportunity and they'll put a cliffhanger out where, okay, and in just a minute I'm going to talk about A, B, and C, but if you're ready to go right now and they'll open the gates there, give people the chance or ready to go to buy. The risk with that, as you said, is you're disclosing price potentially before, as again, bringing up things you said previously, you've said everything you need to say to make the point to establish the value. So there is no one, this is the right way it works. It changes in different markets. It changes over time. So I think as long as you have a good close, you just need to test where it best works in your ad. Yeah, keep thinking about the actual conversation you would have with someone one on one. Are you in a crowded room? Do they only have five minutes? Are you in an elevator? Do you have a lot of time and they've sat down? Are they already inclined to be okay with the price you have? Is the price going to be a shock to them? Do you have to make a bigger case for the price? Is this something that is new in their lives? Is this more of the same? Is this something they've done with other people? Are you being in a heavily competitive market where the competition prices are more or less the same, but you're being different, you're more unique for some reason. All of this stuff comes into play. That's why there's no pat answer for this. But keep bringing it back to that conversation that you're having. Try to think about the situation. Put yourself in the place of the prospect. Is he web surfing and he's more inclined to click off if something turns him off? Has he gone to your site because he wants to find out information? Is this something he's very, very hot on? Is he willing to put up with more or less of the sales process? All of this comes into play. Right. It's also the difference between push and pull. Exactly. Yeah, if you look at, let's say Amazon.com, they can put that buy button right up top. For a couple of reasons. One is you went there looking for something. That is how their marketing is. And almost everything they sell, price won't be the objection. They're generally selling. If you want the information, you want to do that. You know, it costs $20. And they'll give you the information below that. But in that scenario where the person is there to buy, disclosing price earlier and closing the deal is a good move. And if you're in a situation where, now you really need to tell people a story and set them up and justify why this thing is maybe more than $9. Right. The clothes would tend to be after you've made the case, not give people the, oh, that's too much money before they know what they're getting. That's a good point. Right. I think for at this point, I'm sure you're, you're still out there. You're ready to go and write up your clothes. So last step, there'll be an exercise. And then there's one more bonus time and altogether module. Yes. Yes. Okay. All right. It's time for you to put together your clothes. Do one using the one, two punch of selling effectively. Give some rational reasons for your prospect to be able to make the decision now and be aware of the emotional, a brain reasons that you're giving him to make him feel good about it on a very, very deep level.
